name: Tunisian Arabic
notes: Tunisian Arabic (Derja تونسية). Intermediate Maghrebi variety. Vowel reduction significant but less extreme than Moroccan. θ → /t/ (urban Tunis); some rural varieties preserve /θ/. ق → /q/ conservative; /ɡ/ in urban Tunis. Historical Italian/Sicilian contact: /tʃ/ in some loans. Best-documented Maghrebi dialect (Gibson 2002, Talmoudi 1980).

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties

location.notes: Glottolog's representative point for Tunisian Arabic.

stress.notes: Arabic stress is QUANTITY-SENSITIVE: it falls on a syllable because that syllable is heavy, not because of any orthographic ending, so no ending table can express it. The cascade (Ryding, A Reference Grammar of MSA, CUP 2005, §2.3; Watson, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic, OUP 2002, ch.3): a superheavy final syllable (CVːC/CVCC) takes the stress (kiˈtaːb); otherwise a heavy penult (CVː/CVC) does (muˈdarris); otherwise the antepenult (ˈmadrasa). max_onset=1 because Arabic onsets are obligatory and hold exactly one consonant — a medial cluster splits, closing the preceding syllable, and that boundary is what makes the penult of mudarris heavy. INPUT CONTRACT: weight is only recoverable from a complete transcription, so this requires diacritized input; an undiacritized skeleton has no vowels to weigh. The registered wikipron gold carries no stress marks, but the benchmark strips stress from both sides by default, so this costs no PER.
